sections.title: Expressing the future: will/won't and be going to

content.description: There is often little difference between **will** and **be going to** when talking about the future. In many cases, the choice is simply a matter of style: **will** tends to sound more formal, while **be going to** is more typical of everyday spoken English.

content.subtitle: Use
items:
Facts about the future: The new café **will** open on the high street next month. (more formal)
The new café **'s going to** open on the high street next month. (more informal)

Predictions not based on present evidence: One day, people **will** be able to travel to space as tourists. (more formal)
One day, people **are going to** be able to travel to space as tourists. (more informal)

Decisions made at the moment of speaking: I've made up my mind! I **won't** buy a new laptop just yet. (emphasising the decision)
I've made up my mind! **I'm not going to** buy a new laptop just yet. (emphasising the intention)

content.description: In certain situations, **will** is the preferred choice rather than **be going to**.

content.subtitle: Use
items: Offers and suggestions: I**'ll** carry your bags to the car, if you like.
Requests: **Will** you help me carry these boxes upstairs?
Most first conditional sentences: If we leave now, we**'ll** catch the last train.

content.subtitle: Watch out!
content.description: With offers and suggestions in the question form, we do not use **will** with I and we. We use **shall**.
items: ✓ **Shall** I carry your bags to the car?
✗ **Will** I carry your bags to the car?

content.description: In other situations, **be going to** is the more natural choice over **will**.

content.subtitle: Use
items: Plans and intentions (which you already have when you speak): I**'m going to** run a marathon next spring!
Predictions based on present evidence: Look at those clouds! It **'s going to** rain in a few minutes.

content.subtitle: Use
items: Arrangements made before the moment of speaking: Are they delivering the new furniture on Thursday?

content.subtitle: Use
items: Timetables, arrangements and fixed events: The first train to Edinburgh **leaves** at 7.30 tomorrow morning.
After if in first conditional and zero conditional sentences: If the weather stays warm all week, what are we going to do at the weekend?
After certain time expressions: We'll know the results as soon as we get back to the hotel.

content.subtitle: Use
items: Actions which are completed some time between now and a point in the future: I'll have finished cooking dinner by the time you get home.

content.subtitle: Use
items: Actions in progress at a point in the future: This time tomorrow, I'll be lying on a beach in Spain.
Habits or repeated actions at a point in the future: In the future, we'll all be ordering our shopping online using voice-activated devices.

content.subtitle: Use
items: Actions in progress up to a point in the future: At nine o'clock tonight, I'll have been waiting for this concert to begin for two whole hours!

content.description: In time clauses, we avoid using **will** or **be going to** directly after certain time words and phrases. Instead, we use a present tense (present simple, present continuous or present perfect) when talking about future events.

content.subtitle: Use
items: when: It'll be amazing **when** the team **wins** / **has won** the championship.
as soon as: Let me know **as soon as** your parcel **arrives** / **has arrived**.
before: It'll be a few more years **before** they **open** / **have opened** the new airport terminal.
after: Let's grab a coffee **after** we **do** / **have done** the shopping.
until / till: The match won't start **until** the referee **does** / **has done** a final check.
while: Think of me **while** you **relax** / **are relaxing** on the beach!
once: We'll stop for a break **once** we **pass** / **have passed** the motorway junction.

content.subtitle: Use
items: Key prepositions of time: **(from)** Monday **to** Friday, **on** Monday, **on** my birthday/New Year's Day/etc, **in** March, **on** October 15th, **in** 2019, **in** (the) autumn, **at** half past seven, **in**/**for** an hour, **at** the moment, **in** the morning/afternoon/evening, **at** night, **in**/**on** time, just **in** time for, **in** the beginning/end, **at** the beginning/end of, **at** the age of, **at** the weekend, **next**/**last** week

Key prepositions of place: turn left **at** a junction, sit **on** sth, go **in(to)** a building, wait **in(side)** a building, arrive **in** Rome/Italy, arrive **at** the airport, **in**/**on**/**at** the corner (of), come/go/walk/etc **to** a place, **next to**/**beside**/**by** the café, **at**/**on** the front/back of, **in front of**/**behind** the hotel, go **out of** a building, go **towards** the town centre, **between** the two shops, **opposite** the bank

content.subtitle: American English notes
items: Speakers of American English do not always use **on** before days of the week.
US: We've got a football match Saturday/**on** Saturday. UK: We've got a football match **on** Saturday.
Speakers of American English often say 'Monday **through** Friday'.
US: I'm going to be away on a school trip Monday **through** Friday. UK: I'm going to be away on a school trip **from** Monday **to** Friday.

sections.title: A: Will vs. Be going to
sections.instructions: Click the correct option(s). Sometimes both are correct.

items.sentence: Summers **will/be going to** get hotter in many parts of the world over the coming decades.
Careful! You **will/be going to** spill that glass of juice!
I **will/be going to** wash those dishes for you, if you like.
Do you think that, in the future, people **will/be going to** live to be hundreds of years old?
I **will/be going to** open the door for you, if you like.
I know! I **will/be going to** have a big party for my birthday this year!
You **will/be going to** hold this lift door open for me for a second? Thanks a lot!
We've decided we **will/be going to** try that new Thai restaurant after the concert on Saturday.
We **will/be going to** spend our summer holiday in Portugal this year.
We **will/be going to** invite Clara and Ben round for dinner tonight.
You **will/be going to** come to Sophie's farewell party next Friday?

sections.title: B: Circle the correct word or phrase
sections.instructions: Circle the correct word or phrase. If both options are correct, circle both.

items.sentence: **We aren't / We're not** going to be late for the show, are we?
I think **I'm going to do / I'm doing** really well in my job interview tomorrow.
Lucy **is going to sing / is singing** a solo in the school talent contest next Friday.
I'm **going to buy / buying** a flat in the city centre when I save enough money.
The charity fun run is **going to be / being** held on the last Sunday of the month.
**Does / Will** the new shopping centre open before Christmas?
**Are they going to broadcast / Will they broadcast** the football final live on Saturday night?
items.answer: Are they going to broadcast/Will they broadcast

items.sentence: Do they **broadcast / Are they broadcasting** the football final live on Saturday night?
According to the timetable, the coach to Edinburgh **leaves / is leaving** at 9.45.
**Does the restaurant open / Is the restaurant opening** next Saturday after the refurbishment?
items.answer: Does the restaurant open/Is the restaurant opening

sections.title: C: Future forms (gap fill)
sections.instructions: Write a word or short phrase in each gap.

items.sentence: At the end of next month, I'll (1) ...... training for the city marathon for almost eight weeks.
I'll (2) ...... running a twenty-kilometre route on the Saturday after that to prepare for the race.
I (3) ...... going out with friends the evening before either, as I need to get plenty of rest.
I expect I'll (4) ...... following strict training schedules like this throughout the whole season, so it's good to start now.
By the time I finally cross the finish line in three months, I will (5) ...... working towards this goal for nearly six months.

sections.title: D: Future perfect and continuous
sections.instructions: Complete using the correct form of the verb in brackets.

items.sentence: I ...... (cook) the pasta by the time you get home from work.
In a few minutes, I ...... (wait) at this bus stop for over thirty minutes.
items.answer: will have been waiting/'ll have been waiting

items.sentence: The pool closes in half an hour, and I ...... (swim) non-stop for ninety minutes by then.
items.answer: will have been swimming/'ll have been swimming

items.sentence: They ...... (not / finish) renovating the kitchen by the time the guests arrive.
If she's still on her phone at nine o'clock, Jan ...... (talk) to her sister for over three hours.
At five o'clock this afternoon, we ...... (not / climb) the hill for three hours but for five hours!
This time next year, you ...... (probably / pass) your final university exams and be celebrating!
items.answer: 'll probably have passed/will probably have passed

items.sentence: Will you ...... (do) all your packing before we leave for the airport?
By Thursday, Elaine ...... (work) on the new marketing campaign for two whole weeks.
We ...... (probably / not / leave) the party by the time you get there.

sections.title: E: Time clauses (transformation)
sections.instructions: Complete each second sentence using the word given, so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence. Write between two and five words in each gap.

items.sentence:
We'll arrive at the hotel and then we'll unpack our bags. **got**
We'll unpack our bags ...... the hotel.
items.answer: when we've got/once we've got/after we've got

items.sentence:
I'll finish reading the report and then I'll give you my feedback. **soon**
I'll give you my feedback ...... reading the report.

You'll be driving through the mountains next week, so enjoy the views! **while**
Enjoy the views ...... through the mountains next week!

They'll show Titan at the cinema and then they'll release it on DVD. **before**
They won't release the DVD ...... at the cinema.
items.answer: before they show Titan/before they have shown Titan

items.sentence:
My job interview starts at two o'clock tomorrow afternoon. **taking**
At ten past two tomorrow ...... my job interview.
items.answer: I will be taking/I'll be taking

items.sentence:
Let's see the new action film before we have dinner. **watched**
Let's have dinner ...... the new action film.
items.answer: after we have watched/after we've watched

items.sentence:
I'll send out all the party invitations before lunchtime. **have**
By lunchtime, ...... all the party invitations.
items.answer: I will have sent out/I'll have sent out

items.sentence:
Complete the worksheet and then I'll check your answers. **until**
I won't check your answers ...... the worksheet.
items.answer: until you do/until you have done

items.sentence:
When you come to visit us, we'll have redecorated the bedroom. **by**
We'll have redecorated the bedroom ...... you come to visit.

We'll find out the flight details and then we'll make a plan. **once**
Let's make a plan ...... found out the flight details.

sections.title: F: Prepositions of time
sections.instructions: Write one word in each gap.

items.sentence: I'll be 18 years old ...... the year 2027!
Marco isn't going to have a party ...... his birthday this year.
See you ...... December!
See you ...... Friday!
See you ...... half past seven!
See you ...... the afternoon!
See you ...... a couple of hours!
It can be hard to sleep ...... night ...... the summer because of the heat.
She started learning the violin ...... the age of six!
We arrived just ...... time for the opening ceremony.
She's never early or late; she always shows up right ...... time.
My sister's wedding is ...... June 12th.

sections.title: G: Prepositions of place (error correction)
sections.instructions: Each of the words in bold is incorrect. Rewrite them correctly.

items.sentence: My Canadian relatives are coming **in** Spain next summer!
Turn right **on** the traffic lights, then carry straight on.
All the seats were taken, so we had to sit **at** the floor.
The lamp is **on** the corner of the room.
There's a photo of the singer **at** the back cover of the album.
We should arrive **at** Rome by early afternoon.
Could you step out **from** the room for a moment, please?
Walk **in** the bus stop, but turn left about fifty metres before you get there.
I'll meet you **in** the corner of the main square.
There should be a spare key **on** the back of the drawer somewhere.
They should arrive **in** the train station in about twenty minutes.
The remote control should be next **from** the television.

sections.title: H: Cloze
sections.instructions: Write one word in each gap.

items.sentence: The names of great travel eras were only agreed on (1) ...... the era was over, or at least well after those times (2) ...... passed.
(3) ...... the early twentieth century, for example, no one said: 'Tomorrow I'm going (4) ...... fly across the ocean on a commercial flight.'
In the same way, no one ever said: 'Next decade (5) ...... be the start of the railway age.'
(6) ...... the time we are old, we will all (7) ...... witnessed enormous changes in how people travel.
We might even (8) ...... riding in fully autonomous vehicles, or perhaps that technology will have (9) ...... replaced by something even more advanced.
There is growing discussion (10) ...... the moment about sustainable transport networks connecting cities across the globe.
And nobody knows what the era we are living (11) ...... will be called by historians of the future.

If this is already the Age of Low-Cost Travel as some commentators suggest, then what the current era (12) ...... be remembered for remains uncertain, and we are certainly just (13) ...... the beginning of the next great chapter.
